topics: 🛡️ Handing the bull to a שומר — who steps into the owner's place
🔒 Tying it down 'כראוי' — does a tame and notorious bull share guarding standards?
🐂 R' Eliezer: the only שמירה for a notorious is the knife
🐄 The pregnant cow puzzle — who pays for the calf when timing is unknown
🎲 Following majority in monetary cases — the camel-in-heat baraita

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Watchmen, Levels of Guarding, and the Cow's Calf
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: Daf 23 closes Chapter 4 with the topic of handing the bull to a guardian and the level of שמירה required, then opens Chapter 5 with the famous mystery of the gored cow and her calf — a sugya that probes whether monetary law follows majority probability.
overview.hebrewSource: מְסָרוֹ לְשׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם וְלַשּׁוֹאֵל וְלַנּוֹשֵׂא שָׂכָר וְהַשּׂוֹכֵר נִכְנְסוּ תַחַת הַבְּעָלִים.

slides.title: 🛡️ Levels of Guarding
slides.subtitle: R' Eleazar maps the dispute between R' Meir and R' Yehudah onto the שומרים grid.

boxes.title: 🆓 R' Meir
items: Looser standard — easier to be patur

boxes.title: 🔪 R' Eliezer
items: A muad has only one שמירה
Slaughter is the only adequate guard

boxes.title: ⚖️ Practical test
items: Even iron walls aren't enough alone
Personal capacity to watch is decisive

callout.label: Key move:
callout.text: The Yerushalmi reads the Mishnah's dispute about a tied-down ox as a fight over which שומר category sets the bar for damage-prevention.

slides.title: 📊 R' Meir vs R' Yehudah — Bull Damaged or Bull Killed
slides.subtitle: Once the bull is handed over, four scenarios shake out differently.
rows: Unpaid: bull broke out and damaged
Unpaid: bull broke out and was killed/torn
Paid: bull broke out and damaged
Paid: bull broke out and was killed/torn

callout.label: Note:
callout.text: R' Eleazar's astonishment: 'But it was guarded — and the Torah still made him liable!' Resolution: every שמירה in the Torah is measured against the watchman himself, not the wall.

slides.title: 🐄 The Cow With Her Calf
slides.subtitle: A bull gored a cow; her fetus is found beside her.

nodes.content: משנה: שור שנגח את הפרה ונמצא עוברה בצידה
Was the calf already born — or did the goring cause the miscarriage?

columns.label: If doubtful
columns.content: Half נזק for cow + רביע for calf

columns.label: Yerushalmi probe
columns.content: Why not follow רוב — most cows don't miscarry?

nodes.content: Conclusion: monetary law generally doesn't follow majority — but R' Yose cites R' Acha's camel-in-heat case as a counter-example.

callout.label: Yerushalmi tension:
callout.text: זאת אומרת שלא הילכו במידת הדין בממון אחר הרוב — but R' Acha's camel baraita shows they sometimes did.

slides.title: 💬 R' Yannai vs R' Yose — How Does the Half + Quarter Work?
slides.subtitle: Two structural readings of the Mishnah's split payment.

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: R' Yannai: משלם חצי נזק מן הפרה ובעל הוולדות עולה לבעל הפרה רביע — owner pays half from the cow value, and the calf-owner kicks back a quarter to the cow-owner.

lines.speaker: RY2
lines.text: R' Yose: each pays half — half for the cow, half for the calf, treated as separate damage events.

lines.speaker: Q
lines.text: What's the practical difference? מתו הוולדות — if the calves died after the goring.

lines.speaker: A
lines.text: On R' Yannai's reading the cow-owner can say 'I didn't want them — take them as they are.' On the other reading, each pays his own share regardless.

callout.label: Levi's traditions:
callout.text: Reish Lakish concludes: כל אילין שמועתא דלוי אינון — all these readings come from Levi's school.

slides.title: 🐪 The Camel-in-Heat — Following the Rov
slides.subtitle: R' Yose's counter-example to the cow-and-calf principle.

steps.content: 1️⃣ A camel in heat (גמל האוחר) wandered among other camels.
2️⃣ One of them is found dead.
3️⃣ Ruling: the camel-in-heat is liable — בידוע שזה הרגו.
4️⃣ Reasoning: the overwhelming probability is that the agitated camel did it.
5️⃣ Therefore: monetary law sometimes does follow רוב — the cow case is the exception, not the rule.

callout.label: Bottom line:
callout.text: R' Avun bar Cahana asks: if it were certain, would each pay half? R' Immi answers: yes — the Mishnah deals with a muad, and the half is a sale-of-foot logic.

summary.principle: When ownership is delegated, the level of שמירה demanded matches the watchman's category — and where ownership of the damaged thing is split (cow + calf), so is the payment.

flowSteps.content:
Watchmen and split-ownership
Who pays when the cow loses her calf?
Doubt about timing produces split payment — but probability arguments are fragile in monetary law.

🎓 Key Principle:
Liability is shaped both by who was watching and by who owned the damaged property.

quiz.q: According to R' Eleazar, what guarding level does R' Meir require for damages?
quiz.e: R' Eleazar: R' Meir's standard is the שומר חינם level; R' Yehudah requires the שומר שכר level.

quiz.q: How does R' Eliezer say one guards a notorious bull?
o: By chaining it to the ground
quiz.e: אין לו שמירה אלא סכין — the only adequate guarding for a muad is to slaughter it.

quiz.q: In the cow-and-calf case, why doesn't the Mishnah pay full damages for the calf?
o: It's uncertain whether the goring caused the miscarriage
quiz.e: Because of the doubt about timing, the Mishnah pays only a רביע for the calf — half the half-damage.

quiz.q: What does R' Acha's camel-in-heat baraita prove?
o: Monetary law sometimes does follow רוב
Heat is an exempt force majeure
quiz.e: R' Yose cites it to show that in some monetary cases the law does apply majority probability.

quiz.q: What is the practical difference between R' Yannai and R' Yose's readings of the Mishnah?
o: What happens if the calves die after the goring
Whether the cow is muad or tam
quiz.e: On R' Yannai's reading the cow-owner can refuse the dead calves; on R' Yose's, each pays his share regardless.
